sentences.text: That's right.
Well I stood on that the road.
It was in late afternoon at a time when the curfew was on.
And I had to find a place to-- I wanted to get away toward the Russians, because the Russians were already chasing the Germans.
And I wanted to be free of the Germans, because I knew I cannot not survive with them around.
So I headed towards Kiev.
And I stood on the chaussee that led to Kiev.
And nobody would stop.
There were a few cars, of course.
There are not many cars.
But there were horse and carriages.
Anyway, one truck stopped.
It was full of German officers in uniforms.
And they stopped.
They saw me standing there, and they stopped.
So I climbed up on.
There were many of them on the-- this was an open truck.
I climbed up on the truck, and we finished up in a-- in a-- in a German headquarters.
Anyway, I spent the night there.
Nothing happened to me.
They didn't suspect anything.
And nothing happened to me, because I wouldn't allow anything to happen to me.
I slept in a separate room.
And the following day, I continued.
I don't know.
Oh, yes.
I got a lift to some other place, and from there, to Kiev.
And on that-- in that semi trailer that I was traveling in was a woman with a small baby.
It was her sister's child, and the child was dying.
So she was taking the-- the child to doctors in Kiev.
She herself lived in Kiev, but she visited her sister, grabbed the dying the little boy, took-- took him with her to Kiev.
So because she found out I had nowhere to stay in Kiev-- I told her on the truck that I've got nothing to-- nothing-- nowhere to stay.
She said, stay with us.
So the first night in Kiev, I stayed with her.
And the boy died the second night.
So I found myself-- I found myself in a funeral procession through Kiev, after arrival in Kiev-- very soon after.
Anyway, I want to tell you another incident happened in Kiev, very briefly.
I met-- I don't whom I met.
I don't know how I met.
I was invited to some people.
I went in.
They were having a party.
Don't forget there was curfew.
So when it got dark, you couldn't walk out on the street.
And I probably didn't realize that.
Anyway, we were sitting and talking.
And we all stayed there overnight.
All of us stayed in the one big room.
Everybody who attended the party stayed there-- women and men.
There would have been-- I don't know-- about 12 of us.
And I slept on the couch, on the settee, in the middle of the room.
And they were sleeping on the-- on the beds, and on the floor-- on the floor.
Towards the morning, I hear-- I was very uncomfortable.
I hear someone saying-- oh, maybe it was when we-- when the lights were turned off.
I heard a man saying-- saying the following-- she will be dead tomorrow morning, that [NON-ENGLISH], that-- that Jewess.
She'll be dead tomorrow morning.
Now when I heard that, of course I couldn't sleep.
You know, I couldn't fall asleep.
And I had to have a plan.
How am I going to escape this?
I can't go now, it's dark.
You know, somebody on the street would shoot me.
You can't walk in curfew, you know.
It was the war, don't forget.
It was 1943.
The Germans were being chased by the Russians.
It was war.
And the frontier was coming closer and closer to Kiev, because-- you know.
So anyway, so I evolved the plan that I'll leave the shoes, the wooden shoes, under the settee, so they can see it-- in front of the settee.
Because they were sleeping on the floor.
They could see it.
And I'll pretend.
Very leisurely, I'll go to the toilet.
And they will know that I'm coming back, because I left my shoes there.
And I ran for my life.
When the light came on, I sort of leisurely walked out of the room with the shoes on the floor.
And off I went-- when you could-- you know, when the light came on.
And I ran, of course, to this woman there.
This woman put me to her-- she-- she must have realized that I was Jewish.
I don't know.
But she took me to her aunt-- some relative of hers-- whose daughter was taken by the Germans to Germany.
And she was longing for that daughter.
She-- so she thought if she helps me, somebody will help her daughter in Germany.
She was like our own-- my own mother.
She was wonderful.
She was.
And I one day I said to her-- I wasn't there very long.
Because I said to her-- the front was coming closer.
And I said, now listen.
I've got to get to the partisans.
I've got to go to-- I've got to fight the Germans.
I want to go to the partisans.
She said, well, she said, I have a cousin in Berezovka-- next to Berezovka.
And she's in the partisans.
I'll give you a letter.
She gave me a letter.
It was that letter-- I boarded the train.
All the trains were coming loaded to Ukraine and going back empty to get some more loot to bring back.
I was the only on-- this was a goods train, of course.
I went on the steps.
I couldn't climb to the goods thing.
On the steps of the train, I traveled through the Dnieper, towards Kiev.
And I descended in Berezovka.
With that letter-- there was a local shoemaker.
I think she told me about the shoemaker too.
I went straight into the shoemaker.
And he put me in touch with that.
So the following day after I arrived in Berezovka, I was already on my way to the forest where the partisans were.
I arrive with that letter.
The idiotic thing was that I never read that letter.
I don't know what she put in that letter, because it wasn't addressed to me-- although it was about my life!
I never read that letter.
I don't know what was in it.
But anyway, we arrived there-- I don't remember-- with horses and a buggy, or whatever.
We arrived in that forest, and we asked for that woman.
And I had to wait, I remember, quite a long time.
And a young woman emerged.
She would've been in her 20s, late 20s-- came.
And I gave her the letter.
She opened that letter and read it, and looked me over, and read the letter, and said, well, we have got no room for you.
We can't take you.
We can't take you.
I said, what?
She said, we can't take you!
Now to this day-- of course I went back to Berezovka.
I stayed with the shoemaker, by the way, until their Soviets came.
They liberated me in Berezovka.
But to this day, I think there were either two-- two reasons she didn't take me-- number one, because she wanted to save my life.
Because these Ukrainian partisans, some of them were terribly anti-Semitic.
They killed the Jews themselves.
Or, maybe she thought I was too young.
Maybe she thought that I'll get raped by them.
She wanted to protect me.
I think she wanted to-- my opinion was, because her auntie loved me so much, and loved her so much, there must have been something in that letter that told the woman, save that girl.
And she saved me by not letting me join the partisans.
So I went back to Berezovka.
I stayed with the shoemaker and his wife.
And there was a sister there, and a little child.
Anyway, there were-- the Soviets, the-- the Germans were departing.
In fact, to my amazement, the last Germans that left Berezovka saluted me.
Because you see, I was hiding the fact that I was Jewish.
Of course, nobody knew in the village.
Because I also spoke very fluent Ukrainian, don't forget-- very fluent.
I spoke beautiful Ukrainian.
I went to Ukrainian school when the war broke out.
Everybody learned Ukrainian.
Because Poland was gone.
That was Ukraine now.
And I have a gift for languages like nobody's business-- like nobody I know.
I speak 15 languages-- fluently!
I write.
I read, and I speak them.
So of course, they-- they thought I was Ukrainian.
In any case, the point was that there were officers passing.
First there were-- oh, the Germans saluted.
They boarded their horses, and saluted me, you see.
The last ones left on horses.
They didn't have cars anymore.
And they slept in the-- in the-- in the houses around.
I saved Berezovka.
I saved Berezovka.
I just remembered now.
When the Germans left, there was a little Polish guy running.
It was already dark.
The night fell.
He was running with a-- with a light, with a torch.
And he was lighting the house, putting fire to the houses.
And I heard him swear in Polish.
So I got stuck into him, in Polish.
I said, what?
You're going to burn the house of my people here, that I'm staying in?
You-- and I told him off so much that he was scared of me.
And he ran and left the-- all the houses.
He didn't burn them.
And we were standing there, putting water over the houses in case he puts the torch to them.
Because some houses in the outskirts were-- were already-- far away were already burning.
I saved the whole Berezovka.
Because I-- he was Polish-- a Polish little fellow.
He would've been maybe 15, 16 years of age.
He was running with a torch, burning houses after the Germans left the village.
Anyway, so shortly after I went to Kiev, shortly after Kiev was liberated, you see-- this is now September, more or less, '43, that we're talking about.
Kiev was liberated in January '44-- oh, maybe, no-- earlier.
Because I went there early '44.
I went to Kiev.
So Kiev must have got liberated soon after Berezovka.
But I waited probably a month or so.
And-- and then I went to Kiev.
And-- aha.
One of the officers who passed through Berezovka was a Jewish fellow who was hurt-- had a-- he was an invalid.
He had two sons in the army and a wife somewhere in Russia-- Jewish guy.
And he had epilepsy.
I didn't know it at the time.
But anyway, he was-- he was an invalid.
And he was going back to Kiev, because he was from Kiev, originally.
And he left me his address.
He said, when I'm-- when I'm-- reach Kiev, you know, I'm going to Kiev.
When I reach Kiev, if you ever come to Kiev, here is my address.
So when I went to Kiev, in early '44, of course I went straight to this invalid.
He-- he received me with open arms.
And then he had a epileptic fit on the floor.
And I never saw a fit in my life.
So I ran to the street.
I wanted people to help.
I thought he was dying.
I've never seen an epileptic fit.
Anyway, I stayed with him.
And then-- and then his wife came from Russia.
And he went-- he was an invalid, completely not an invalid.
In any case, he stayed.
His wife came with a lover from Russia-- long story.
Old people-- old people we're talking about.
Anyway, so then I stayed in-- I stayed in Kiev.
And I finished school in Kiev.
And I enrolled in a medical institute in Kiev.
And I studied there.
And I payed two visits to Moscow to visit my-- my uncle came, my mother's brother, whose address I remembered.
I never met him in my life-- never saw him in my life.
Somehow or other, someone mentioned the address during the Soviet occupation.
Someone mentioned it.
He came to visit his mother, and his sister, who was my mother, and that other sister in Kovel.
But I never met him, you see.
I never met him.
Anyway, he came to Lutsk-- to Lutsk-- to Kiev, fetched me, went to Moscow.
And I stayed there for the summer holidays.
Then I came back.
And I went there twice to Moscow and stayed with them in summer, for the holidays.
He want-- they want-- he wanted me to stay in Moscow.
But they were on the ration cards.
He had all the documents ready, because he was a very important guy.
He was the chief accountant for the railways for the Soviet Union.
He worked opposite the Kremlin.
In any case, so what shall I tell you?
Oh yes.
In '44, there I am Kiev, hungry, living in a cold little one room and a kitchenette.
That fellow already went to-- he got a big apartment for his wife-- prepared a very big apartment for his wife and her lover.
I don't know what happened there anymore.
I can't remember what happened.
I didn't keep touch with them that much.
And then I attended a medical institute.
In Russia, everybody-- how did I support myself?
I have no idea how I supported myself.
There was-- you-- study was free.
They give us, I think, a soup.
Later on, I moved to the students' house, you see.
And they gave us a watery soup with a potato floating there.
And I went to the market and bought apples.
And that's how I lived.
We shared, in the-- in the-- in the-- in the student's house, five girls lived in one room.
Five girls lived in one room.
And we had potato soup and a slice of bread.
And I bought apples in the market.
And that's how I lived.
I didn't need money.
What did I need money-- money for?
I was studying all day, coming home, had the soup, and bed.
Anyway, then my friends came from-- the dentist in Lutsk, his son was very much in love with me.
And I really had a crush on him too, when we were children, when we were very young teenagers.
And he and his mother-- his mother had a sister in Kiev.
And they came to Kiev.
And they found out.
I don't know whom they found out from, that I'm in Kiev.
And they searched me out.
And they waited for me until I passed my exams.
And we left Kiev together by train.
No.
When I was liberated.
Oh, I didn't tell you.
In Berezovka, the day I was liberated by the Russians, by the Soviets who came, oh!
When the Soviets came, my heart broke.
They were-- they were walking.
They were tired.
They had no boots.
They had no food.
They were not shaven.
They had nothing.
There were thousands of them wandering.
I saw the Germans was, you know, departing on horses, and clean uniforms.
Here come the Russians, walking like dead mice.
It was heart breaking.
And they were liberating me, and people like me.
I shall never forget it.
No matter what they say about the Russian army, I will never forget them.
They saved me and people like me.
I'll never, never forget it.
I owe them my life, my survival.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --from Kiev we went by train to Poland.
In Poland, I stayed only a few days.
I couldn't bear it.
Because it reminded me of the anti-Semitism, of the inhumanity during the ghetto time.
And during the-- the whole-- the whole memory of Poland became such a painful thing, that I-- I just couldn't stay in Poland.
My friend and his-- his mother stayed in Wroclaw.
We got a beautiful apartment by the way in-- in Western Poland-- beautiful apartment, the three of us.
They came to Kiev because his mother's sister lived in Kiev.
They came to visit her.
But they also knew that I'm there.
So they waited for me for about two months in Kiev, so that I finished my exams.
Because he was hoping to marry me.
His mother was mad about me.
She knew my parents.
Don't forget, this is prewar.
I mean, there is a prewar connection.
We were school children together.
Not-- well, he was two classes-- he was about the age of my sister.
So he was about two years older.
He lives in Scotland now.
I'm very often in touch with them.
I visited them in Scotland not so long ago.
Anyway, so I didn't stay in Poland.
My cousin who survived the war in Siberia waited for me in Poland, grabbed me by my arm, [NON-ENGLISH] by one arm, and the other one by the other arm.
And I had to make a choice.
I went with my cousin.
We-- we got through the frontier, semi officially-- semi officially.
A lot of people were leaving Poland at the time.
We jumped.
Because in Kiev, I was studying, remember-- from January '44.
Was high school, finish--
--finishing high school--
--and university.
They didn't-- medical institute-- the number one-- there was only one medical institute in Kiev at the time.
And with, you know, the war was still on, until '45.
So we never had summer holidays.
We studied nonstop.
So we did-- not three semesters a year.
We did four or five semesters a year.
Because they need the doctors for the army.
So they were training us nonstop.
So in two years, two and a 1/2 years, I did three years of medicine.
In two and a 1/2 years, I did three years of medicine, because I left in July-- in June '46.
And I started-- I finished school early '44.
So I had two-- just over two years and did three years.
But I had to-- three years of medicine in two and 1/2-- just over two years-- not only I, everybody else did too.
I have-- anyway, when I came to Vienna, I had to repeat all the exams.
I remember I had to-- I came to Vienna with this cousin of mine, right?
We're on the train.
And some girls found out-- there were other people on the train.
We were walking through the border same-- in-officially, but in-officially.
But the fellow on the Czech/Polish border, Polish/Czech border, said to me, oh.
You should stay in Poland.
You're such a pretty girl.
You should stay in Poland.
You shouldn't go away.
You shouldn't leave Poland.
They knew.
They was-- they were escorting us.
It was approved by the government, that whoever wanted to leave can leave.
Anyway, we didn't stay in Czechoslovakia.
We had to pass by train, Czechoslovakia-- finished up in Vienna.
And there were people on the train.
There were a few Jews on the train.
I don't know who they were.
I can't remember who they were.
I don't know who they were.
But they got in touch with some-- maybe I asked them?
I don't know.
They got in touch with-- two women came running-- two girls came running to the railway station, took me off the train-- me and-- and I, of course, take to my cousin.
The were from Lutsk.
They used to come to Kiev-- to visit me in Kiev.
They were already engaged to two fellows in Vienna.
Anyway, they took me off the train.
I stayed with them.
My cousin wouldn't stay in Vienna.
He went to the camp in Salzburg.
He survived it all in Siberia, remember?
That was-- it was that auntie from Kovel-- his parents who lived in Kovel where I went to high school.
Right.
He was the only one that survived, because he left with the Russians.
He didn't wait for the Germans to come in-- in-- in '41.
He-- he blew it!
He had a twin brother who stayed behind.
The whole family was killed.
He was the only one from my mother's side, except for the Soviet brother, Uncle Alexander, that I told you who lived in Moscow, who survived!
This only fellow-- and he's got two children now.
And-- and one lives in Toronto, his daughter, and the son lives in Washington, DC.
And I attended the wedding.
The photo you saw, that was last year.
He got married, and I was at the wedding.
So what shall I tell you now?
That we were finished-- we finished up in Vienna.
OK.
I stayed in Vienna.
I got an apartment in Vienna.
I studied in Vienna.
I enrolled in university.
I had to repeat exams.
I even had to repeat a Latin exam.
And we were not taught Latin in Russia.
But we were taught Latin at the high school in Poland, in the first-- first-- class of gymnasium.
So when the professor in Vienna asked me to come to his house to have an examination-- and when I opened my mouth, when he asked me questions, he couldn't believe that I know-- knew so much Latin!
He expected someone who studied in Russia not to have any idea of Latin, because Latin was not taught in Russia, you see.
In any case, I was a student in Vienna.
I graduated in Vienna.
And after I graduated in Vienna-- in Vienna, I had the best life of my life, I had in Vienna.
The best-- I had-- oh, but in Vienna, I made the decision.
You see, when I came to Vienna, I was surrounded by Austrians.
Who were the Austrians?
They were like the Germans!
They were Nazis!
They were followers of Hitler.
And I found myself at university together with them-- these handsome, blond, beautiful guys.
A lot of them were ex-servicemen, not all of them as young as I.
There were a lot of ex-servicemen, people who were already in their twenties, in they-- around 30-- wearing these Tyrolean outfits-- beautiful looking fellows.
And I couldn't-- I knew that I can't-- they-- I knew that they were my enemy.
I knew they were killers!
And I had to study with them.
But I had a girlfriend, Christina.
I studied with Christina, who was a Polish Jewess.
She was married already to [NON-ENGLISH].
They survived the war.
They were Holocaust survivors.
And we studied with her together.
We met.
We went to-- we sat at the lectures together.
When I came home, I made a decision, in the very first few weeks of my attending university, that I cannot live with hatred in my heart.
It'll destroy me.
If I am going to live in the city, and study at that university, I cannot hate.
So I decided right there and then I am going to forgive them today!
But I am never going to forget it.
And I never told anyone about it.
It was my own private resolution with myself.
I solved the problem.
I forgave them!
Do you know what it means to forgive the Germans in '46-- one year after the end of the war?
On my-- on my own bet-- without anyone, without consultations, without any advice, without discussions-- it was an inner process.
I had to forgive them, because I cannot live with hatred.
It'll destroy me.
I won't have a life.
And I-- I wont give anyone a life.
Around me they want-- I cannot.
So I forgave them, but I-- they never-- I never invited any of the students home.
I mixed with Jewish students only.
In fact, I was the public relations.
My boyfriend-- I fell in love in Vienna.
I lost my virginity in Vienna.
I fell in love with Vienna, with Alex.
And he was the president of the Jewish students.
And they voted me as a PR person for the Jewish students.
And I begged them not to, because I was a very serious student.
I am a very serious person.
And I am very interested in knowledge, in spirituality.
I always have been.
So anyway, when they wanted to make me the PR person, I said, listen.
I have to study.
I have exams to pass.
Don't choose me.
They insisted.
Anyway, so there I was, representing the-- the-- the Jewish students of Vienna University.
But I was studying.
I graduated in 1949.
I have these-- these graduation photographs here.
I studied very hard.
But I-- but I had a wonderful life.
I had a circle of friends.
All the place, all the musicals, all the theaters-- not only saw them with friends, but discussed them with friends.
It was-- it was paradise.
It-- Vienna is a beautiful city.
Some of my friends, one friend in particular, had a car-- was a chauffeur.
Every weekend we drove around.
It was the best life I've had so far, was in Vienna.
It really was fantastic.
Anyway, after I graduated, I had to make a decision.
There was no need for me to stay in Vienna.
Vienna was not my town.
I had nothing to-- to-- to do in Vienna, really.
I got-- I was there because to get my degree.
By the way, I want to mention to you that before the war, my parents said that we will be professionals.
One of us will be a lawyer, and one of us will be a doctor.
One of us, Lola, will study in Paris.
And I'll study in Vienna, or vice versa.
This was my parents' plan that I overheard as a child.
And strange enough, after they got killed, didn't exist anymore, their wish was carried out by me.
But Uncle-- Uncle Adolph, my father's brother, wasn't there anymore.
He finished up in Argentina, where he died in Buenos Aires.
He survived the war.
He ran away from Hitler.
And his daughter is still living there.

What I heard that happened to Lola was-- we lived in a small little room, which was a part of an apartment, in which there was-- to get to our room from the-- through the kitchen, you had to walk through a room of a doctor, who lived there with this mother, and an auntie, and an uncle.
This was in Lutsk, in the ghetto--
--in the ghetto-- in the ghetto.
There was a third room in which lived some tradesmen.
I don't exactly know what tradesmen they were.
But it was a family with two or three sons.
And we all shared the same kitchen.

I understand from people who survived, that just before they liquidated the ghetto in Lutsk, they wanted gifted tradesmen to keep them longer alive, so that they can benefit; so they-- the tradesmen can do jobs for the Germans and for the Ukrainians.
And I understand that my sister found myself.
And I suspect that one of these tradesman's sons took her along with him to that castle.
Remember that medieval castle had a courtyard in the middle?
That's where they took the tradesmen to.
And somebody told me that Lola was seen in that castle, still alive with the tradesmen, but not-- but they liquidated them all right there.
And I don't know when.
Yes, they were all killed.
But I don't know whether she was killed in between the 22nd and the 25th of August like the rest of the ghetto, or whether she was killed a few weeks later.
This I don't know.
But I suspect that because the tradesmen were there, sharing that kitchen, the apartment-- a beautiful family-- beautiful family-- the mother was such a darling woman.
Look.
Honestly, I mean, we-- you see, I suspect that my sister went with one of these boys who was chosen as a good tradesman, to go to the castle.
And she probably went along with him.
Never saw her, I never saw, never heard, never, never, never, and all my girlfriends that went to the high school, like, you know-- never, never saw them, never heard of them-- the Jewish ones.
Nobody ever came back-- nobody.
The only handful that survived, like these two girls from Lutsk, that came to-- that took me off the train-- they were hiding separately, not together.
And they were-- they met after the war-- after the war.
And they met two fellows who were partisans-- together-- Jewish fellows, from some village under Lutsk.
They finished up marrying them.
And the wedding was at my place in Vienna-- after I got my apartment already in Vienna-- a ex-Nazi apartment I got.
And the ex-Nazi woman was knocking on my door every second day-- could she come and live with me, and she'll cook for me.
And I just, of course, wouldn't play along with her, for God's sakes.
But-- because the apartment wasn't hers.
It was a Jewish apartment.
And eventually, the proper owner came from the United States of America-- Nelly-- Elly Nebor-- came with a bunch of flowers, and said to me, look.
I-- I'm living in a hotel.
And this-- I lived in before the war, here in this apartment with my parents.
But I've just come from New York.
My sister is still in New York.
But we've come to live in Vienna now.
Can I come and share the apartment with you?
And that was a two bedroom apartment with a huge, big sitting room in the middle.
I said well, it's big enough for both of us.
But you know what happened?
After, her sister came with her husband and a child.
I had to leave the apartment.
They were communists, these two women.
They were communists.
And this was in the Soviet zone.
They went to the-- to the Soviet fellow and told them they wanted the apartment.
So I had to leave.
I saw her in Vienna, just now, a few months ago.
She's still there in that apartment-- Nelly-- Elly.
Well, I mentioned to you before that my father survived the war in Austria, in Vienna.
When he came-- the first world war-- when he came to Poland the early '20s, he met my mother in Lublin.
Because my grandfather lived in Lublin.
Originally, my grandfather was not from Lublin, but he was, during the first war-- he-- they lived in Lublin, anyway.
So my father met my mother in Lublin, fell in love with her.
And they married.
And Lola was born soon after.
And then, of course, I came along.
Lola was born in '22.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a couple that never argued.
My father was so much in love with my mother that-- and she was just-- they never-- I've never heard an argument in the house.
Maybe that's why I didn't argue with Lola, because I've never heard people arguing-- argue in my life-- until the war, of course-- until, you know, I saw people kill each other.
But I've never-- I've never-- I never known that-- I've never know that people can argue.
It was such a harmonious, quiet place, wonderful place.
For instance, in Lutsk, we used to go to high school, come back home at 3 o'clock, or shortly after, a few minutes after.
We used to have dinner together and discuss everything under the sun.
There was my mother.
There was my father at the head of the table.
There was me and Lola.
And the maid used to bring the dishes, and you know, three or four courses.
And one-- and the discussions, and-- and then my father used to lie down, have a little sleep, and then he used to take off and go again somewhere.
I don't know where.
And you know, and we went to do house-- homework or whatever, and or play with friends.
So it was a very, very sedate, very normal, very beautiful, harmonious life-- very beautiful life-- very beautiful life-- family life-- very beautiful family life.
Very good relationships.
There was never any friction.
I remember once, I was angry with my mother, because she didn't let me go to a party, because I had a cold.
So I thought, what a terrible mother I've got.
She's not letting me go to-- to a party, because I have a cold.
That was the only incident I remember that I was against my mother.

Through having lost your family so early in your life, how-- how did you actually cope with-- with the thought that you may never see them again, in all the times that you were hiding, all the experiences that you went through?
Look.
I can't describe the trauma, the shock that one gets.
Because I went to Lutsk, don't forget, in '44 already, to see whether anyone survived.
I went when the front was still on.

In fact, the night I arrived, and went back to the solicitor and his wife, with my little bundle with extra shoes and change of clothes, I had to leave, because the Germans took over the-- the town again that night.
So I had to leave.
And I came back in '45-- in '45 again, to see the grave, and to meet the handful of people that survived that are in this picture here-- that I'm going to show you shortly.
What-- you're asking me how did I cope with it?
First of all, I had to-- the first period of time, I had to fight for my survival.
I had no time.
I knew that I had no one in the world-- no one in the world.
I had no right to live.
I had no right to exist!
I had no right to exist.
I was supposed to be dead.
If I was alive, it was by mistake.
Because if I had poke-- poked my head out of the door, and went in the street, I would've been shot.
Look, I didn't tell you that when I was hiding at the Schmigero's place, there was a Jewish fellow, an uncle of this Hiaca girl that-- that-- they had--
--that was killed.
--they had a-- they had a brewery.
And there were several Schneiders.
There were-- one family, Schneider, but there were a lot of brothers and cousins.
One of these Schneiders, was Erik Schneider.
And he was hiding with me-- an old man.
Where I was hiding on the--
--on top of the house.
The identity card
Yes.
But Erik Schneider hid with me on top of the house there for a little while.
One day he went to the street-- and he was beloved by everybody.
He was-- he didn't look like a Jew.
He was a playboy.
He loved women.
He wore a little bow tie.
He played cards.
He was not the Jewish industrialist.
He never worked.
You know, he was a playboy.
He was loved with-- the women loved him too.
No.
But he went to the street, and he got killed.
That's what I wanted to say-- that if I would have poked my head out from this-- from the-- from the hiding places that I was in, I would have finished up like the rest of them.
Somebody in the street would have recognized me, and killed me-- as-- recognized me as being Jewish.
